title: The Complete Operator Guide to Backup Restore
author: Veltrix
excerpt: The parameters that matter most, the mistakes that compound, and the implementation sequence that avoids both from an infrastructure operator perspective.

content.text: The single biggest production risk for any live Hytale server is the loss of critical world data or the collapse of a critical component of the server’s architecture, whether due to player‑generated corruption, an accidental wipe, hardware failure, or a bug in a mod. A sudden data loss can crush player trust, break economies, and force an emergency shutdown that stops revenue for days, while a slow, cobbled‑together restoration can leave players stranded and disrupt scheduled events. Therefore, every operator must treat backup survival as first‑class infrastructure, not a nice‑to‑have feature. In this guide we will focus on the day‑to‑day practice of pulling a fresh snapshot from your backup store, dropping the old files, validating world integrity against the latest patch, and re‑starting the Hytale process without compromising uptime or data safety. Our goal is to give you afail‑proof, repeatable routine that lets you react to disaster in minutes, not hours.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

Search traffic shows operators turning to “how to restore Hytale backup,” “restore files from server backup,” and “reload Hytale server” only after launch, because the initial launch phase focuses on setup, configuration, and ensuring backups are created; queries spike once players report issues or data loss, prompting administrators to recover. To restore a full Hytale backup, stop the server, replace the current world folder with the backup copy from the backup zip or tar archive, and verify checksum. After restoring the directory, edit the serverconfig.yml to match the correct world name and root folder path, then restart the server to load the restored world. If only specific files need restoration, copy those files directly into the appropriate subfolders (e.g., /worlds/[worldname]/data, /plugins, or /worldborder) while the server is offline, preserving permissions. Reloading a server after a partial restore is done with the in‑game command /reload or by sending the signal SIGHUP to the server process; this refreshes all plugins and caches without a full restart, minimizing downtime.
The Architecture Decisions That Determine How Well You Scale

The way you structure the backup and restore stack defines the scalability of a Hytale server network. Deploying a distributed object store for raw block backups, instead of a single NFS mount, eliminates a single point of contention and lets new nodes pull data across the cluster with minimal lock contention. Coupling these backups to immutable snapshot layers written into a stateless container image adds a versioned checkpoint that can be pulled and fast‑started by any node. This approach eliminates the need to transfer large files during restores, leaning on the container runtime’s copy‑on‑write layer for divergent state. For load‑balanced restore, use a lightweight API gateway that routes restore jobs to the least‑loaded node, maintaining a buffer queue in a message broker so that the network does not see spikes. In production, asynchronous replication of patch logs across data centres guarantees that even after a catastrophic failure, replicas stay within milliseconds of each other. The critical architectural decisions—selecting a high‑throughput object store, employing immutable layered images, and decoupling restore workloads from the game engine—directly impact how many concurrent restore operations can run without degrading live traffic. Integrating these patterns from the outset ensures horizontal scaling, reduces restore latency, and maintains overall service availability.
Database and Proxy: Getting the Foundation Right

For a stable Hytale backup restore strategy the first priority is ensuring the database and proxy layers are correctly aligned. Before applying any restore, confirm the running instance’s PostgreSQL or MySQL version matches the backup payload; a minor version mismatch often leads to schema incompatibilities that will corrupt restored worlds. Use pg_restore or mysqldump’s --single-transaction flag to guarantee atomicity, and always run the restore in a staging environment to validate integrity by executing SELECT COUNT(*) queries against key tables like player_info and world_state. On the proxy side, dedicate a separate Nginx or HAProxy configuration handling only Hytale traffic, with sticky sessions bound to the player’s UUID for consistent routing. Configure load balancers to perform TCP health checks on the exact UDP port Hytale uses, and set fallbacks to drain nodes gracefully before rerouting. Keep the proxy configuration version-controlled and apply changes through a blue‑green deployment pipeline; after a backup restore, recycle the proxy to flush any stale routing tables. For production, lock the database in read‑only mode during the restore to prevent race conditions, and post‑restore, run a quick checksum against uworld.zip and related map files to ensure no corruption slipped through. This disciplined alignment of database schema and proxy routing protects against deadlocks, session loss, and stale data during a full restore.

To restore a full Hytale backup you should first stop the server process and back up any current world data. Then locate the latest backup file, usually a .zip or .tar.gz stored in the /backups folder or in the root if you use the default Naming scheme. Unzip or untar the backup to a temporary folder, overwrite the existing world folder and the server configuration with the versions inside the backup. After that start the server with the same command used for regular launches and confirm the world loads correctly. If you encounter errors, check that the backup was made with the same server build version.  

Restoring specific files from a backup is similar but more selective. Extract the backup file to a temp directory, navigate to the files you need—such as world/*.db files or settings/*.json. Copy those files over the live versions with the same directory structure, ensuring you preserve ownership and permissions. If the server is running, stop it before copying to avoid file locks; after the copy finish, reboot the server to apply the changes.  

Reloading the Hytale server after a restore can be done with a simple restart. Send a STOP command through the RCON interface or run the shutdown script, wait until the process exits, then execute the start script (./start.sh or your service unit). Alternatively, if you use systemd, issue `systemctl restart hytale`. Ensure the process ends cleanly; if it hangs, kill it with `kill -9 $(cat /var/run/hytale.pid)` and start fresh.  

Network misconfigurations often cause unexpected packet loss during restore, but the simple trick of disabling default NAT reflection and binding the server to a dedicated IP resolves most connectivity hiccups before a full reload.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

One common mistake is misconfiguring the bind address, using 0.0.0.0 or a public IP when the server should only listen on localhost, which exposes the server to unwanted traffic and can be exploited by attackers. Another frequent error is setting an incorrect port range; providing a port lower than 1024 without root privileges or letting the firewall block the chosen port (e.g., 8000) prevents remote connections and results in “cannot be reached” errors for clients. Incorrectly ordering firewall rules—placing a deny rule before an allow rule for the Hytale service—causes legitimate traffic to be dropped, leading to connectivity outages that appear as server crashes. Finally, overlooking DNS resolution or using the wrong host entry in the hosts file can redirect player traffic to the wrong IP; this results in a “server not found” error and wasted bandwidth on unnecessary connections. Each of these misconfigurations can cause server downtime, player loss, and, in the worst case, expose sensitive data to intruders.
Questions Operators Are Asking Right Now

To restore a Hytale backup you first ensure your server is stopped; use the control panel or send a “/stop” command on launch. Then locate the backup archive (typically a .zip or .tar.gz in your backups folder). Copy this archive to the same directory on the host, and extract it with “unzip backup.zip” or “tar -xzf backup.tar.gz” preserving the folder structure so that the worlds, config files, and mods replace the current ones. Verify that the data folder is correct and that permissions are set to the service user (chown -R hytaleuser:hytaleuser serverfolder). For restoring specific files from the backup, open the archive in a utility and drag the desired assets or configuration files into their target directories; again double‑check permissions. Once extraction is complete, restart the server via the panel or “systemctl start hytale” if using systemd. Reloading the server can also be done with the in‑game command “/reload” which clears any loaded plugins and reads configuration files anew without fully stopping the VM; this is useful after minor adjustments. If you encounter any missing Java requirements, reinstall the appropriate JRE first. The overall process is a straightforward stop‑extract‑start cycle that maintains your server state without data loss.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

First log into the control panel and pause the Hytale service to prevent file locks. Then download the latest backup package from the off‑site cloud bucket or the internal SAN, ensuring the SHA256 hash matches the manifest. Third mount the backup VHD to a temporary drive on the host and copy the main world folder and essential config files over the current “worlds” directory, preserving permissions; verify that worldlist.json references the restored files. Fourth update the router’s NAT rule to point port 19132 to the server’s LAN IP, and if using split tunneling, add an exception for that port in the firewall. Fifth run the server jar with “--enable-backup-restoration” to allow it to load from the temporary checkpoint before shifting to the primary state. Sixth audit the logs for any loading errors and, once satisfied, restart the service. Lastly, send a FINAL ping from a test client to confirm packet receipt, then restore the CDN cache to push the new state to all clients.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

The most critical network parameter during a Hytale backup restore is the available upload bandwidth; a gigabit connection is recommended for a 10‑GB server snapshot, ensuring the transfer completes in under ten minutes at optimal conditions. Directly following upload capacity, latency must stay below 50 ms for the server controller to periodically ping back confirmation packets; higher ping leads to timeouts and failed restores. The TCP window size should be set to at least 5 MB per stream on both ends so that the large packets from a burst of data can be acknowledged promptly without stalling the pipeline. MTU settings need to be aligned to 1500 bytes on all network segments; any fragmentation will trigger packet loss that scrambles the restore order. Packet loss itself must be under 0.1 % throughout the transfer; a higher loss rate forces retransmissions that double the effective data volume. Quality of Service (QoS) policies must prioritize the backup traffic over gaming packets to avoid CPU thrashing on the server machine. Finally, DNS resolution latency should be minimal (under 20 ms) so that the server can re‑establish its domain mapping after the restore; sluggish DNS prolongs the reload window, leading to client connection delays.

To restore a Hytale backup on a production server, stop the daemon with `/etc/init.d/hytale stop` or `systemctl stop hytale`, ensure all sockets are closed, and copy the desired backup archive to the server's data root `/var/lib/hytale`. Use `tar -xzvf backup.tar.gz -C /var/lib/hytale` to extract the files; overwrite existing world, config, and player data. Verify permissions with `chown -R hytale:hytale /var/lib/hytale`; correct any mismatches that might prevent startup. Once extracted, start the server again with `/etc/init.d/hytale start` or `systemctl start hytale`. The server will load the restored data automatically.  

If you need to restore specific files from a backup, first locate the file within the backup tarball by listing: `tar -tzf backup.tar.gz | grep path/to/filename`. Extract only that file with `tar -xzf backup.tar.gz path/to/filename -C /var/lib/hytale`. After extraction, adjust ownership and permissions as before. Relying on partial restores can lead to data inconsistencies; prefer full restores for critical data like world chunks.  

Reloading the server after changes without a full restart is possible with the in-game command `/reload` issued by a player with operator privileges. This will re-read configuration files and reload resource packs but does not reload the world data; world chunks are refreshed on server tick. For a complete reload of world state, stop and restart the process.  

Sharp insight: In high‑traffic, long‑term Hytale clusters, network drivers must be tuned for low‑latency packet handling; missing TCP window scaling or inadequate MTU padding can throttles throughput, leading to gradual server lag that manifests only after months of sustained load.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

As a Hytale server scales beyond hundreds of concurrent players and begins approaching the 1,500–2,000 range, the network stack demands deliberate reconfiguration. First, funnel all inbound traffic through a hardware load balancer or a cloud‑based auto‑scaling group that distributes UDP packets across multiple front‑end VMs, ensuring each handles no more than 8,000 packets per second to avoid packet loss. Increase the MTU to 1500 bytes for jumbo frames but enable GSO and GSO offload to reduce CPU overhead. Tune the TCP window scaling to 64 KB per connection and enable BBR congestion control on the backend to bolster throughput. Apply QoS policies that tag Hytale UDP traffic with priority 5, guaranteeing it bypasses lower priority background maintenance packets. For DNS, shift from single‑point resolution to SRV record round‑robin values that weight by server reputation and latency, pushing more traffic to the closest datacenter. Scale the CDN edge caching to store static assets, reducing RTT from 200–250 ms to sub‑80 ms for global players. Finally, implement flow‑control thresholds that throttle writes to 70 % of total available bandwidth once the sample packet drop rate exceeds 1 %, preventing raid‑level congestion and ensuring a responsive game experience.
Related Topics Worth Understanding

When operators search for “Hytale restore backup,” “How to save Hytale server,” or “Does Hytale save automatically,” they are usually confronting a data‑loss scenario, misconfigured persistence settings, or an unexpected crash that wiped recent world changes. Hytale writes out new world files every five hours by default, but any manual backups must be created ahead of halting the server or after a major update. If an admin is struggling to restore a backup, it signals a possible oversight in backup scheduling or a failed restore script. The only way to recover lost data is to locate the most recent valid backup, stop the server, move the current world folder out of the way, copy the backup into the world directory, then restart the server. Operators should verify auto‑save settings in the server config, ensure backups are taken regularly, and maintain a clear workflow for restoring to avoid downtime.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

The backup file must be present on the network share that the server can reach via SMB or NFS and the share must be mounted at boot with the correct credentials; the Hytale server process must be stopped gracefully by issuing the shutdown command to ensure file system consistency and to lock the world data; the DNS record for the server’s hostname must resolve to the public IP that the Allowlist in the igws firewall permits on TCP port 6969 and UDP port 6969 for lobby and play traffic, otherwise clients will not reach the server; the local router must have NAT‑Passthrough enabled for port 6969 so that outbound authentication requests reach the validation service; the firewall on the host must allow outbound HTTPS traffic to the asset servers over TCP port 443 for asset download after a restore; the server’s locale time must be synchronized to an NTP server to keep the timestamped backup files correct; the backup compression tool must have write access to the temporary restore directory on the local disk; the Hytale server log rotation must be disabled during a restore to avoid disk churn; the backup metadata file must match the current world version or you will be forced to resync the map; and finally, once the restore files are in place, the server must be relaunchable by invoking the start script without errors, indicating that the world data and configuration files are intact.

To restore a Hytale backup, first secure the server by stopping the service – `systemctl stop hytale` or `screen -X -S hytale quit`. Verify the backup directory under `/var/backups/hytale/`. Use `cd /var/backups/hytale/` then list archives with `ls *.tar.gz`. Extract the desired snapshot with `tar -xzf snapshot-YYYY-MM-DD.tar.gz`. The archive should contain three primary directories: `world`, `config`, and `players`. Copy each to the live server path, preserving permissions: `cp -a world /opt/hytale/server/`, `cp -a config /opt/hytale/server/`, and `cp -a players /opt/hytale/server/`. After extraction, run `chown -R hytale:hytale /opt/hytale/server/` to set correct ownership. To restore individual files, locate the file within the extracted snapshot, copy it over the live version, then reload its permissions similarly. Example: `cp /opt/hytale-backups/world/world-1.dat /opt/hytale/server/world/`. Once the files are in place, reload the server with the command specific to your setup: if using systemd, `systemctl restart hytale`; if using screen, detach the screen session, then `screen -S hytale -X acquire` or run `screen -dmS hytale java -Xmx4G -jar server.jar`. Monitor `/var/log/hytale/` for start-up prints. Finally, confirm cloud sync or any external services are pointing to the correct location.  

Sharp insight: A properly configured firewall must explicitly allow both IPv4 and IPv6 traffic on UDP/TCP port 27275; misrules cause intermittent packet loss that looks like server lag and hampers backup sync.
